# trovex — full reference for LLMs

> trovex is the canonical doc store for AI coding agents. It indexes a repo's markdown and
> serves an agent the single current doc that answers a query — a `path:line` pointer with a
> freshness marker — instead of letting it reread the repo to guess which file is canonical.
> About 60% fewer tokens per doc lookup, measured at equal task-success (methodology at trovex.dev/measure). Open source, local-first
> (SQLite + ONNX), runs on your machine. Ships as an MCP server and a CLI.

- Website: https://trovex.dev
- Access: public beta — the repository is public; install from https://github.com/TsukumoHQ/trovex (a star helps)
- Repository: https://github.com/TsukumoHQ/trovex (public)
- Community: https://tsukumo.ch/slack (questions and setup help)
- License: core AGPL-3.0, CLIs MIT
- Category: context / memory infrastructure for AI coding agents
- Interface: Model Context Protocol (MCP) server (local, streamable-HTTP at http://localhost:8765/mcp) + CLI

---

## What trovex does

- **Serves one canonical answer per query.** For a question, trovex returns the single current
  doc that answers it (down to the section), with a freshness marker — canonical, stale, or
  duplicate — not a pile of candidate chunks for the agent to sift.
- **Section-level reads.** It hands back the paragraph that answers, not the whole file, so a
  short answer costs short-answer tokens.
- **Shared write path.** One agent records what it learns once (an incident, a decision, a fix);
  every other agent and your teammates read it back — a single source of truth by construction,
  with nothing to sync.
- **Local-first.** Indexing and embeddings run on your machine in SQLite + ONNX. No cloud, no
  API keys, your code never leaves your machine.

The only performance number trovex claims is about **60% fewer tokens per doc lookup**, measured at equal task-success (median 69% across a 26-query benchmark on our own repo, range ~41-81%, LLM-judged; we headline a conservative ~60%); full methodology and a reproduce-it-yourself harness at trovex.dev/measure — one
canonical answer versus reading the top few stale candidate files. It's open source and in
public beta; there are no customer counts or ratings to cite.

## Who it's for

- Solo developers and small teams (1–20) running AI coding agents on real, doc-heavy repos.
- Anyone whose agents reread the same `.md` files every session and burn tokens guessing which
  doc is current.
- Teams who want several agents (and teammates) working from one current source of truth.

## Install and run

```
uv run trovex index /path/to/repo   # index your markdown
uv run trovex serve                 # serve MCP at http://localhost:8765/mcp, UI at /
```

## Connect your MCP client

trovex is a local streamable-HTTP MCP server at `http://localhost:8765/mcp`. Any spec-compliant
MCP client that supports HTTP servers can connect.

- **Claude Code:** `claude mcp add --transport http trovex http://localhost:8765/mcp`, or add
  `{ "mcpServers": { "trovex": { "type": "http", "url": "http://localhost:8765/mcp" } } }` to
  `.mcp.json`. Verify with `/mcp`.
- **Cursor:** add `{ "mcpServers": { "trovex": { "url": "http://localhost:8765/mcp" } } }` to
  `.cursor/mcp.json`, enable in Settings → MCP.
- **Windsurf:** add `{ "mcpServers": { "trovex": { "serverUrl": "http://localhost:8765/mcp" } } }`
  to `~/.codeium/windsurf/mcp_config.json`, refresh in Cascade.
- **Cline:** add `{ "mcpServers": { "trovex": { "type": "streamableHttp", "url": "http://localhost:8765/mcp" } } }`
  via the MCP Servers panel (`cline_mcp_settings.json`).

(Client field names move between versions — check the client's current MCP docs if it doesn't connect.)

---

## How trovex compares

### trovex vs CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md
CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md are one static markdown file the agent loads in full every session.
They're great for a short, stable set of project rules. They go stale, don't scale past a
handful of topics, and can't route a question to the right doc or section. trovex indexes many
docs and serves the one current doc that answers a query, with a freshness marker. Keep a small
CLAUDE.md for rules; let trovex carry the corpus.

### trovex vs repomix / files-to-prompt
repomix and files-to-prompt pack your repo (or a subset) into one big prompt — the right tool
for a one-shot question about a whole codebase. For repeated, targeted lookups they flood the
context window on every prompt, which is the opposite of token-efficient. trovex retrieves the
one current doc that answers, so a short answer costs short-answer tokens.

### trovex vs context-hub (CTX) / chunk-retrieval servers
RAG-style context servers return a ranked list of candidate chunks and leave the agent to sift,
rank, and judge which to trust — with no freshness signal and usually no write-back. trovex
returns one canonical doc with a canonical/stale/duplicate marker, and lets agents write back
what they learn so the fleet shares one source of truth.

---

## Answers to common questions

### How do I stop my AI coding agent from rereading the same files every session?
Give the agent a single canonical answer per query instead of raw files to sift. Index your
repo's markdown once, then serve the one current doc that answers a question — a `path:line`
pointer with a freshness marker — rather than letting it reread files to guess which is
canonical. That cuts the cost to about 60% fewer tokens per lookup, because the agent reads one
current section instead of the top few stale candidates. trovex does this as a local MCP server.

### How do I reduce the token cost of giving a coding agent context?
Cut the repeated retrieval, not the one-time prompt. Most of a coding agent's context cost is
paying again and again to reread the same docs to find which is current. Route each query to the
single canonical doc that answers it, read at the section level, and skip stale and duplicate
copies. Together that's about 60% fewer tokens per doc lookup.

### What is canonical context for coding agents?
Canonical context is the single current, authoritative document that answers a coding agent's
question — served with a freshness marker, instead of a pile of candidate files the agent has to
sift. Canonical means the one doc that is the source of truth for a topic, as opposed to stale or
duplicate copies. The benefit is lower token cost (about 60% fewer per lookup) and more reliable
answers, because the agent is not reasoning over outdated copies.

### Does a bigger context window make rereading docs cheaper?
No. A bigger context window raises the ceiling on how much you can load, not the efficiency of
loading it. You still pay for every token, on every session, across every agent — so rereading
the same docs is exactly as expensive per token, you can just do more of it. A bigger window is
also not a more current one. The durable fix is to load less but the right thing: one canonical
doc per query, about 60% fewer tokens per lookup.

### How do I keep multiple AI agents working from the same docs?
Give every agent one shared point to both read and write, instead of separate copies each
maintains. When one agent records what it learns through a single store, every other agent and
teammate reads that record back instead of re-deriving it. With one point of passage for reads
and writes there are no copies to drift and nothing to sync — a single source of truth by
construction. trovex implements this with a shared write path.

### What's the difference between agent memory and retrieval (RAG)?
Agent memory is what an agent retains about its own past — its working context during a run, plus
stores of past events (episodic), facts (semantic), and how-to (procedural). RAG fetches the
top-k most similar chunks from a corpus at query time. Memory is recall of your own history; RAG
is on-demand lookup by similarity. They solve different problems, and neither one gives a fleet of
agents a single current source of truth: per-agent memory drifts, and RAG ranks by similarity so
it returns stale and duplicate copies alongside the right one. The missing piece is a canonical
context layer — one shared store that serves the single current, authoritative doc per topic with
a canonical/stale/duplicate marker, that every agent reads from and writes back to. trovex is that
layer; it sits alongside an agent's memory and any RAG stack, not in place of them, at about 60%
fewer tokens per lookup.

---

## FAQ

**Why not a CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md?** Those are one static file. They go stale, don't scale
past a handful of topics, and can't route a question to the right doc or section. trovex keeps
many docs canonical and serves the one that answers.

**My context window is huge. Why bother?** The cost compounds: every session, every agent, every
teammate. And a big window isn't a current one. trovex serves the right doc, cheaply, every time.

**Is it another service to run?** One local process. Run `trovex serve`, point your agent at it,
and you're set in about a minute. No cloud, no keys.

**Does my code leave my machine?** No. Indexing and embeddings run locally in SQLite and ONNX.
Nothing is sent anywhere.
